questions.text: It _ me a lot of money.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Costs is the present tense, while we are looking for the past.
The sentence is in the singular first person. "Cost" is used to "have as a price" when referring to something that has happened in the past so, option B is the right fit here.]
"costed" is not a standard verb form in English.
"Is costing" is in the present tense and so as a result this option is eliminated.

questions.explanation: The sentence is in the singular first person. “costs” refers to all of the pieces within that group (being plural), hence incorrect. "costed" is the past tense and the past participle of cost and means to estimate or determine the cost of something hence incorrect. "cost" is used to "have as a price" or "cause to lose, suffer, or sacrifice” in past tense and past participle hence correct. ‘Is costing’ is in the present tense and hence incorrect.

questions.text: My mother, along with others, _ worried.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Were is used for third person plural past tense e.g they, we and for the second person past tense e.g you. So as a result this option is omitted.
Are, when used in the sentence above doesn't make sense grammatically. Also are is used for plural nouns.
Have is used for first and second person singular and plural present tenses and for the third person plural present tense. So, this option is not correct.
First we are to recognize that this sentence is in the past tense. So, was is used in the past tense for third person singular. Hence, the correct option is D.

questions.explanation: As the sentence is in the past tense (the word “worried” is used), “was” is correct as it is in the past tense used for the third person singular. “were” is used for both the third person plural past tense (they and we) and the second person past tense (you) hence incorrect."Are" is used to describe plural nouns however does not make grammatical sense when the sentence is said out loud. "Have" is used for the first and second-person singular present and plural tenses and the third-person plural present tense hence incorrect.

questions.text: Twenty years _ the minimum age to fill this form.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Are is used for plural nouns and hence is a misfit here.
The subject here is a singular noun so, the correct word to fill this blank should be is. Hence, making option B the correct answer here.
The sentence is not in context to the third person singular so, has is not used here either.
Have is used where one expresses possession of something which is not required here at all.

questions.explanation: As the subject is a singular noun (the word ‘form’ is used), the missing word should be "IS".As the sentence is not in the third person singular, "has" is not used. ‘Are’ is used for plural nouns, and ‘have’ is used to express possession, ownership or acquisition, which is not required in this context.

questions.text: I bought _ umbrella to go out in the rain.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A being an indefinite article is used before consonant sounds. Hence, this option is not correct.
The word umbrella begins with a vowel sound "u" so the indefinite article, an is used here. So, B is the correct option here.
The being a definite article would not be a suitable answer here. Therefore, this option is also eliminated.
The sentence will make no sense and will have grammatical errors if no article is used. The sentence will remain incomplete hence, this is not the correct option.

questions.explanation: The indefinite article "an" is used before singular countable nouns that begin with a vowel sound. Therefore, "an" is appropriate here because "umbrella" starts with a vowel sound ("u"), and the speaker is referring to any umbrella in general.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with a suitable word;The two machines _ considerably. One has an electric motor, the other runs on oil.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Different means to be non-identical or incompatible. Also, this word makes no grammatical sense when used in the sentence above.
Differentiate means to transform, evolve or alter. And so this word is a misfit here.

In the sentence above, two machines are being compared on how they are being run. The suitable option to use here is C, differ. Differ means to be unlike or dissimilar. This word has the correct usage here as it makes sense grammatically.
Difference also means dissimilarity or variation but in this context, it is incorrect and hence cannot be used.

questions.explanation: In this case, the two machines are being compared based on how different they are (on the premise of the way the two machines are run). ‘Differ' means to be unlike or dissimilar and in this context makes grammatical sense and hence is correct.‘

questions.text: I have already got a _ at a hotel in Murree. We shall have no problems.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A prescription is a written order from a medical professional for a medication or treatment plan. Hence, this option is eliminated from being used.
Property is something that is owned by a person or group, such as a house, land, or possessions. This word also makes no sense to be adjusted here and therefore is incorrect.
The reserve is a supply of something kept for future use, or an area of land kept for a particular purpose. As a result, this option is also not correct.
Reservation is to have an arrangement to have something reserved, such as a hotel room, airline ticket, or table at a restaurant. So, option D is the correct answer here.

questions.explanation: Reservation is to have an arrangement to have something reserved, such as a hotel room, airline ticket, or table at a restaurant. So, option D is the correct answer here.

questions.text: Which verb is NOT in the present tense?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The presence of -ed creates a past tense in the sentence.
"Talks" is in the present tense, describing an action that is currently happening.
"Waits" is in the present tense, describing an ongoing action or state.
"Eats" is also in the present tense, describing a habitual or repeated action.

questions.explanation: The presence of -ed creates a past tense in the sentence.

questions.subject: English

options.explanation: After “did not,” the main verb must be in its base form, not past tense. So it should be “accompany”, not “accompanied.”
After “do not”, the main verb must be in its base form, not past tense. So it should be “accompany”, not “accompanied.”

The subject “I” correctly pairs with the base form of the verb “accompany” using do not for negation.The sentence is in the present simple tense, correctly expressing a habitual or general action, which is appropriate here.

This is grammatically incorrect."Accompanied" is in the past tense whereas "accompany" is in the present tense. For the sentence to sound correct when read out aloud and to be grammatically correct option C is correct.

questions.explanation: The subject “I” correctly pairs with the base form of the verb “accompany” using do not for negation.The sentence is in the present simple tense, correctly expressing a habitual or general action, which is appropriate here.

questions.text: Which verb is NOT in the future tense?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The sentence is in the future tense.
The sentence is in the future tense.
The sentence is in the future tense.

Something happening now is in the present tense which usually ends with a 's. When there is a probability of something happening in the future it is known as the future tense. For future tense words such as will and shall are used. So, the future tense is not used in option D hence it is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: Something happening now is in the present tense which usually ends with a 's. When there is a probability of something happening in the future it is known as the future tense. For future tense words such as will and shall are used. So, the future tense is not used in option D hence it is the correct answer.

questions.text: Identify the word similar in meaning to the given word:Innumerable
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The word "limited" means that there is a specific amount or quantity of something, or that there are restrictions or limitations on what can be done.
"Weary" means feeling physically or mentally tired, often as a result of hard work or a difficult situation.
"Innumerable" means too many to be counted or too numerous to be counted. Therefore, the correct option is C.
"Harmless" means not causing any harm or danger, or not likely to cause any harm or danger.

questions.explanation: Innumerable means too many to be counted. "Countless" means too many to be counted; very many. As innumerable have the closest meaning to "countless" (synonyms), it is the correct answer.

questions.text: Identify the sentence structure.How ridiculous this is!.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A declarative sentence is a sentence that makes a statement or declaration. It is used to convey information or to express a fact, opinion, or idea. It ends with a period (.) So this option is not the right fit.

An imperative sentence is a sentence that gives a command or makes a request. It is used to tell someone what to do or to make a suggestion. It usually ends with a period (.) So this option is a mismatch here.

An interrogative sentence is a sentence that asks a question. It is used to obtain information or to clarify something. It usually ends with a question mark (?) to indicate the speaker's tone of voice. So this option is eliminated.

An exclamatory sentence is a sentence that expresses strong emotion, such as surprise, excitement, or anger. It usually ends with an exclamation mark (!) to indicate the speaker's tone of voice. Therefore, the right option is D.

questions.explanation: The sentence is "exclamatory" as strong feelings/ emotions are expressed and an exclamation mark is used. As the sentence does not have a command/ order hence it is not "imperative". It is not "declarative" as it does not express strong emotion with information. It is not "interrogative" as no questions are asked in the sentence and are not exclamatory as no strong feelings/ emotions are expressed.

questions.text: If mountains are _ of trees, rains will soon wash the fertile topsoil down the slope to end as useless silt below.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Deforested means the removal of trees, which leads to soil erosion as the topsoil is washed away by rain.
Afforested refers to the process of planting trees, which is the opposite of deforestation. It helps prevent soil erosion.
Stripped can mean removing something, but it is less specific than 'deforested' in the context of tree removal and its environmental impact.
Shortage refers to a lack of something, but it does not specifically describe the removal of trees or its consequences.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Deforested.' When mountains are deforested, the removal of trees leads to soil erosion. The topsoil, which is fertile, is washed away by rains, resulting in silt accumulation below. 'Afforested' is incorrect as it means planting trees, which prevents erosion. 'Stripped' is too general and doesn't specifically refer to tree removal. 'Shortage' does not relate to the context of environmental impact due to missing trees.

Possession can also refer to the physical control of something, such as when a person is holding an object or has it within their reach. Hence, the correct spelling lies in option B.
This is the wrong spelling.

Possession can also refer to the physical control of something, such as when a person is holding an object or has it within their reach. Hence, the correct spelling lies in option B.

questions.text: She will _ her lesson for two months next week.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Has learnt is in the refers to an action in the past and so it is incorrect.
The sentence is in the present continuous tense. And so she has been learning makes sense when read out aloud but more importantly, it is grammatically incorrect. So option C is the correct option.
'Have been learning' incorrectly uses the present perfect continuous tense, indicating an ongoing action that will continue into the future.
'Has been learns' is grammatically incorrect because 'learns' cannot follow 'has been'. The correct form is 'has been learning'.

questions.explanation: The sentence refers to an action that started in the past and will continue into the future, next week, requiring the present perfect continuous tense. The correct form is 'has been learning', as it matches the singular subject 'She' and the future context.'Has learnt' is incorrect due to its past perfect tense, not aligning with the future context. 'Have been learning' is incorrect because 'have' does not agree with the singular subject 'She'. 'Has been learns' is grammatically incorrect, as 'learns' cannot be used in this tense structure.

questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Write" does not show the continuous tense of the sentence hence is incorrect.
The helping verb "am" is missing and so the sentence is incomplete and incorrect.
The sentence is in the present continuous tense as the word 'now' is used. I am writing is the most suitable answer here as "I" indicates singularity. So option C is selected here.
"Wrote" does not show the continuous tense of the sentence hence is incorrect.

questions.explanation: As the sentence is in a present continuous tense (the word ‘now’ is used), ‘writing’ is the most suitable option. As the subject ‘I; is singular, ‘am’ is used.

questions.text: Your friend _ (wait) for you for over an hour.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Wait is grammatically incorrect to be used here because it uses with present tense.
Waiting is a present continuous verb, but the sentence is in past tense.
The action has been carried out in the past. And in the options listed only the word "waited" is in the past tense hence C is the correct option.
Waits is in the present tense.

questions.explanation: The sentence is in the past tense, and "waited" is in the past tense hence correct. Wait doesn't make grammatical sense when the sentence is said out loud, "waiting" is a present continuous verb and waits is in the present tense.
